text: Okay, so this is a post-hysterectomy sacral copepaxi. So this patient's uterus was removed years ago, but she has vaginal vault prolapse and including an inner seal.
The surgeon begins the sacral dissection by making a small incision in the peritoneum overlying the sacrum, ensuring the incision is distal to the bifurcation of the common iliac arteries.
The surgeon uses a PK device to press down and identify the location of the sacral promontory, searching for a window between the fatty tissue layers rather than cutting directly through them.
The surgeon navigates through the presacral fat, which appears as finger-like projections, to find small windows that allow access to the anterior ligament.
The surgeon exposes the anterior longitudinal ligament during the rectopexy procedure, revealing its location amidst the presacral fat.
